name: Sandalwood
medicinalUse: Heartwood oil treats skin disorders, anxiety, and urinary tract infections. Paste used for cooling fevers. Central to Ayurvedic Chandanadi preparations.
ecologicalRole: Semi-parasitic tree that draws nutrients from host roots, strengthening soil networks. Provides dense shade. Seeds dispersed by Malabar giant squirrel.
sacredAssociation: Sacred to Vishnu and Shiva. Paste applied on deity idols. Used in havan offerings. Symbol of purity and devotion across all Karnataka traditions.
funFact: It takes 30-40 years for sandalwood to develop its fragrant heartwood. Karnataka state tree and protected by law.

name: Banyan Tree
medicinalUse: Aerial roots used for dental hygiene. Bark decoction treats diabetes. Latex applied for skin cracks and wounds. Leaves in poultice for inflammations.
ecologicalRole: Keystone species supporting 87 vertebrate species. Aerial roots create new trunks expanding forest cover. Year-round fruiting supports birds, bats, and primates.
sacredAssociation: Represents immortality; associated with Lord Vishnu. Couples circumambulate banyan for marital prosperity. Village panchayat meetings traditionally held under it.
funFact: The Great Banyan in Kolkata has an aerial root spread of 156 meters — wider than a stadium. Karnataka's sacred groves preserve ancient specimens over 400 years old.

name: Peepal Tree
medicinalUse: Bark treats leucoderma and diarrhea. Leaves boiled for treating jaundice. Root bark used for gum disease. Fruit powder for asthma.
ecologicalRole: One of only a few trees producing oxygen at night. Provides year-round food for fig-specialist birds and wasps. Host for 50+ epiphyte species.
sacredAssociation: Lord Buddha attained enlightenment under a Peepal tree (Bodhi tree). Sacred to Hindus, Buddhists, and Jains. Never cut in sacred groves — considered house of spirits.
nativeDistricts: All Karnataka districts, especially common in sacred groves
funFact: The Peepal is the only tree that releases oxygen 24/7, making it the most beneficial tree for urban air quality. Its fig-wasp mutualism is 60 million years old.

name: Neem
medicinalUse: Leaf paste treats skin diseases. Bark decoction for fever and malaria. Oil for fungal infections and head lice. Seeds for natural pesticide. Twigs as toothbrush.
ecologicalRole: Natural pesticide — limonin from leaves repels 200+ insect pest species. Deep taproot accesses subsoil water. Provides dense cooling shade. Fast-growing carbon sequester.
sacredAssociation: Sacred to Goddess Mariamma and Sheetala Devi. Neem leaves adorn doorways during festivals. Goddess idols decorated with neem branches during temple processions.
nativeDistricts: Dakshina Kannada, Uttara Kannada, Dharwad, Belagavi
funFact: Every part of the neem tree has medicinal value — it is called 'Village Pharmacy' in traditional knowledge. One neem tree can protect 200 sq meters of crops from pests.

name: Tulsi
medicinalUse: Leaf tea for coughs, colds, and fevers. Reduces cortisol (stress hormone). Antimicrobial essential oil. Treats respiratory disorders. Adaptogenic properties.
ecologicalRole: Strong aromatic volatile oils repel mosquitoes and insects in surrounding area. Attracts honeybees for pollination. Enhances soil microbiology.
sacredAssociation: Most sacred plant in Hindu tradition. Considered manifestation of Goddess Lakshmi. Every household and grove maintains a Tulsi plant. Evening prayer ritual (Tulsi Puja) conducted daily.
nativeDistricts: All Karnataka districts — cultivated in every sacred grove
funFact: Tulsi purifies air more effectively than most houseplants, releasing antimicrobial compounds. NASA research confirms its air-cleaning properties.

name: Wild Orchid
medicinalUse: Tuber powder (Salep) treats respiratory conditions, intestinal disorders. Used in traditional strength tonics. Flower paste for skin glow in folk traditions.
ecologicalRole: Epiphyte requiring pristine forest conditions — acts as indicator species for forest health. Pollinated by solitary bees and long-tongued moths.
sacredAssociation: Considered divine flowers offered to forest deities. Used in deity garlands during Navaratri. Their rare blooming is considered auspicious sign.
funFact: Wild orchids are indicator species — their presence proves zero pesticide use. Karnataka's sacred groves protect over 80 wild orchid species found nowhere else in the world.

name: Indian Rosewood
medicinalUse: Bark extract treats skin diseases. Heartwood decoction used for digestive issues. Leaf paste applied for healing wounds in folk medicine.
ecologicalRole: Nitrogen-fixing tree that enriches soil. Dense canopy supports large epiphyte communities. Hollows in old trees provide nesting for hornbills.
sacredAssociation: Considered sacred in forest deity worship. Traditional musical instruments (veena) crafted from wood with devotional significance. Timber never harvested from sacred groves.
funFact: Indian Rosewood is one of the world's most valuable tropical hardwoods. Sacred grove protection has preserved this vulnerable species in Karnataka while it vanished from unprotected forests.

name: Bamboo
medicinalUse: Young shoots for digestive health. Bamboo silica (tabasheer) for respiratory ailments. Burned ash for dental care. Leaf tea for fever reduction.
ecologicalRole: Fastest-growing plant — sequesters carbon 3x faster than equivalent trees. Root system prevents soil erosion on stream banks. Dense thickets shelter small mammals.
sacredAssociation: Sacred in folk traditions as Lord Krishna's flute material. Bamboo groves protect boundary of sacred groves. Used in all festival decorations and ritual poles.
nativeDistricts: Widespread — Kodagu, Shivamogga, Uttara Kannada, Chikkamagaluru
funFact: Bamboo can grow 91cm in a single day. Its tensile strength rivals steel, making it the strongest plant material known. Sacred groves preserve 40+ native bamboo varieties in Karnataka.

name: Teak
medicinalUse: Bark treats skin diseases and blood disorders. Flower decoction for urinary problems. Seed oil promotes hair growth. Leaf extract anti-inflammatory.
ecologicalRole: Deciduous tree creating seasonal food availability through leaf fall. Large leaves serve as mulch enriching soil. Habitat for Indian giant flying squirrel.
sacredAssociation: Ancient teak trees in sacred groves are considered 'witness trees' to historical events. Used in ritual construction of temple vehicles (rathas) and divine palanquins.
funFact: Teak wood can last 1000+ years without rotting when properly seasoned. Karnataka's sacred groves contain naturally-grown teak trees 300+ years old that represent the original forest cover.

name: Malabar Giant Squirrel
medicinalUse: No medicinal use. Protected species — important in traditional folklore as forest messenger.
ecologicalRole: Primary seed disperser for large-seeded trees including sandalwood, rosewood, and wild mango. Nest building aerates forest canopy. Helps regeneration of forest gaps.
sacredAssociation: Considered divine messenger in Kodava tradition. Its call is believed to alert villagers to presence of predators and strangers near sacred grove.
nativeDistricts: Kodagu, Chikkamagaluru, Shivamogga — endemic to Western Ghats
funFact: Malabar Giant Squirrel can leap 6 meters between tree branches. Its fur has three distinct colors in a single coat — maroon, cream, and black — making it India's most colorful mammal.
